extension.valueMarkdown:
## Character
57-year-old Korean-American man, owner of a small dry cleaning business. Lives in Minneapolis, MN with his wife Soon-Yi. Generally healthy, moderate alcohol use (2-3 glasses of wine per week with dinner). Active — plays golf in summer, walks regularly. Insurance: employer group plan through a local business association.

## Clinical Scenario
Kevin has been seeing his PCP for routine care — well-controlled mild hypertension (lisinopril 10mg) and borderline cholesterol (managed with diet and exercise, no statin). In November 2025, he develops intermittent palpitations and mild exertional dyspnea while golfing. He dismisses it initially but mentions it at a routine follow-up in January 2026. His PCP discovers an irregularly irregular pulse at 94 bpm. A 12-lead ECG confirms atrial fibrillation.

His PCP starts workup: TTE (echocardiogram) shows mild left atrial dilation (4.3 cm), preserved LVEF (58%), no valvular disease. TSH is normal. CBC and metabolic panel are normal. CHA₂DS₂-VASc score is 1 (hypertension). His PCP initiates rate control (metoprolol succinate 50mg daily) and anticoagulation (apixaban 5mg BID), then refers Kevin to an electrophysiologist at the University of Minnesota for evaluation of catheter ablation.

The EP specialist at U of M issues a Permission Ticket to access Kevin's records from his PCP, including the ECG, echocardiogram, labs, and medication history relevant to the consult.

## Sites
- **Minnehaha Internal Medicine** — PCP in Minneapolis, MN (2018–present). Routine chronic disease management, AFib discovery, initial workup, rate control + anticoagulation initiation.
- **University of Minnesota Cardiac Electrophysiology** — EP specialist in Minneapolis, MN (2026). Consultation for catheter ablation evaluation, Holter monitor, shared decision-making.

Two sites, one state (MN). Tests Permission Ticket constraints on **scopes** (specialist needs cardiac-relevant data: ECG, echo, labs, medications — not unrelated history) and **organizations** (community PCP → academic specialist).

resource.name: University of Minnesota Cardiac Electrophysiology Clinic
University of Minnesota Cardiac Electrophysiology Clinic

managingOrganization.display: University of Minnesota Cardiac Electrophysiology Clinic

note.text: Refined characterization at EP consultation: AF classified as likely persistent based on continuous AF on ECG and approximately 3 months of ongoing symptoms. EHRA symptom class IIb (symptoms affecting daily activity). Ablation candidacy assessment initiated. Mild LA dilation (4.3 cm), preserved LVEF (58%). Rate controlled on metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily. CHA2DS2-VASc 1, on apixaban 5 mg BID.

2026-02-22: 48-hour Holter monitor confirms persistent atrial fibrillation with 100% AF burden. No periods of sinus rhythm identified over the entire recording. Reclassification from 'likely persistent' to confirmed persistent AF. Adequate rate control (avg 72 bpm), no significant pauses, minimal PVC burden (0.3%).

2026-03-09: Follow-up consultation reviewing Holter results with patient and spouse. Symptomatic despite adequate rate control (exertional dyspnea, reduced exercise tolerance). Shared decision-making discussion regarding catheter ablation with pulmonary vein isolation (PVI). Patient elects to proceed with ablation as rhythm-control strategy. Pre-procedure workup initiated (pre-op labs, TEE/cardiac CT for LA anatomy and thrombus exclusion, anesthesia clearance).

interpretation.text: Persistent atrial fibrillation confirmed; no periods of sinus rhythm identified

note.text: Occurred during brisk walking at 10:42 on 2026-02-19. Brisk exertional response but within acceptable limits.

interpretation.text: Rare isolated PVCs, no couplets, no ventricular tachycardia runs; low arrhythmic risk

note.text: 47 hours 42 minutes. Adequate recording duration, signal quality adequate throughout.

Completed 2026-02-20. Results interpreted 2026-02-22: persistent AF confirmed with 100% AF burden.

resource.conclusion: Persistent atrial fibrillation with 100% AF burden. Adequate rate control at rest (avg 72 bpm) on metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily. No clinically significant bradycardia or pauses. Minimal ventricular ectopy (PVC burden 0.3%). Findings support classification as persistent AF and are consistent with patient's symptom profile.
